seoTitle: Allegro MicroSystems Products | Current Sensors, Motor Drivers | Distributor
seoDescription: Complete catalog of Allegro MicroSystems current sensors, magnetic sensors, and motor driver ICs. Technical specifications and selection guide for automotive and industrial applications.

faqs.question: What types of current sensors does Allegro offer?
faqs.answer: Allegro offers a comprehensive portfolio of Hall-effect current sensors: 1) Isolated current sensors (ACS series) - provide galvanic isolation up to 4.8kV with integrated current-carrying conductor, ideal for high-voltage applications; 2) Non-isolated current sensors - for low-voltage applications where isolation is not required; 3) High-bandwidth sensors - up to 1MHz bandwidth for AC current monitoring and fast fault detection; 4) High-precision sensors - ±1% accuracy for metering and monitoring applications; 5) Digital output sensors - I2C/SPI interface for microcontroller integration; 6) Automotive-grade sensors - AEC-Q100 qualified for -40°C to 150°C operation. Current ranges span from ±2.5A to ±200A, with various package options including SOIC-8, QFN, and custom packages for busbar mounting.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our Current Sensors category to find isolated, non-isolated, high-bandwidth, and automotive-grade options.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Allegro motor driver?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Allegro motor driver depends on your motor type and application requirements: 1) Motor type - BLDC (A4931, AMT49105), stepper (A4988, DRV8825), or brushed DC (A4950); 2) Power level - integrated drivers for < 500W, pre-drivers with external MOSFETs for higher power; 3) Control interface - parallel, PWM, or SPI/I2C control; 4) Sensor requirements - sensorless BLDC control available, or Hall sensor inputs; 5) Protection features - overcurrent, thermal, undervoltage protection; 6) Automotive qualification - AEC-Q100 options for vehicle applications; 7) Advanced features - FOC, sinusoidal commutation, speed control loops. For automotive fuel pumps and fans, the AMT49105 is ideal. For industrial stepper applications, consider the A4988 or DRV8825.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our Motor Driver selection guide or contact our FAE team with your motor type, power level, and control requirements.

faqs.question: What magnetic sensor technologies does Allegro use?
faqs.answer: Allegro employs multiple magnetic sensing technologies: 1) Hall-effect sensors - measure magnetic flux density perpendicular to chip surface, mature technology with excellent reliability; 2) TMR (Tunnel Magnetoresistance) sensors - higher sensitivity and lower power than Hall-effect, ideal for precision applications; 3) Vertical Hall technology - measure in-plane magnetic fields for 2D/3D sensing; 4) CVH (Circular Vertical Hall) - proprietary technology for high-resolution angle sensing. Sensor types include: linear position sensors (A1324), angle sensors (A1335, AAS33001), speed sensors (ATS series), and 3D magnetic sensors (A31301). Applications range from throttle position sensing to motor commutation and gear tooth detection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Hall-effect for general applications; TMR for high-sensitivity requirements; angle sensors for rotary position measurement.

faqs.question: What is the difference between isolated and non-isolated current sensors?
faqs.answer: Isolated current sensors (ACS series) provide galvanic isolation between the current-carrying conductor and sensor circuitry: Advantages - safe high-voltage measurement (up to 4.8kV isolation), no external isolation needed, compact integrated solution. Use for: high-voltage power supplies, motor drives, solar inverters, EV battery management. Non-isolated current sensors measure current without galvanic isolation: Advantages - lower cost, smaller size, lower power consumption. Use for: low-voltage applications (< 60V), battery-powered devices, cost-sensitive consumer products. Key selection criteria: Working voltage - use isolated if > 60V; Safety requirements - isolated for touch-safe designs; Cost - non-isolated for budget-sensitive applications. Allegro's isolated sensors integrate the current conductor, providing the simplest solution for high-voltage applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use isolated sensors for high-voltage (> 60V) applications; use non-isolated for low-voltage, cost-sensitive designs.

faqs.question: Does Allegro provide evaluation tools for their products?
faqs.answer: Yes, Allegro provides comprehensive evaluation tools: 1) Evaluation boards - dedicated PCBs for each product family with test points and connectors; 2) Demo software - GUI applications for sensor configuration and data visualization; 3) Reference designs - complete system designs for typical applications; 4) Application software - motor control firmware, sensor calibration tools; 5) Simulation models - SPICE models for circuit simulation; 6) Magnetic design tools - calculators for magnet selection and placement. Evaluation boards include: ACS evaluation kits for current sensors, motor driver demo boards with motors, LED driver evaluation systems. LiTong Electronics stocks popular evaluation boards and can order specific kits for your evaluation needs. Contact our FAE team for evaluation support and application guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong for evaluation board availability; request demo software and reference designs from our FAE team.

categories.name: Current Sensors
categories.description: Hall-effect current sensors with isolation, high bandwidth, and automotive qualification for EV, industrial, and power applications.
categories.longDescription: Allegro MicroSystems is the world's leading supplier of Hall-effect current sensors, offering the industry's most comprehensive portfolio of isolated and non-isolated sensing solutions. Available through authorized distributor LiTong, Allegro's current sensors integrate the current-carrying conductor and Hall sensor in a single package, providing galvanic isolation up to 4.8kV in compact SOIC packages. These sensors offer significant advantages over traditional shunt-based sensing: no external isolation required, < 1mΩ conductor resistance for minimal power loss, bandwidth up to 1MHz for AC monitoring, and ±1% accuracy for precision applications. The portfolio spans current ranges from ±2.5A to ±200A, with automotive-qualified options (AEC-Q100 Grade 0) for the harshest environments. Applications include EV battery management, motor drives, solar inverters, and power supplies.
series: ACS37610 - High-current busbar sensor for EV
categories.selectionGuide: Select isolated sensors for high-voltage applications; consider bandwidth for AC monitoring; choose accuracy based on metering requirements.

faqs.question: What is Hall-effect current sensing?
faqs.answer: Hall-effect current sensing uses the Hall effect to measure current without physical contact. When current flows through a conductor, it generates a magnetic field proportional to the current. A Hall sensor placed near the conductor measures this magnetic field and outputs a voltage proportional to the current. Allegro's integrated sensors combine the current conductor and Hall sensor in one package. Key advantages: galvanic isolation (no electrical connection between current path and sensor), bidirectional sensing (measures current in both directions), low power loss (conductor resistance < 1mΩ), and fast response (< 2μs). The Hall voltage is V_H = (I × B) / (n × t × e), where I is current, B is magnetic field, n is charge carrier density, t is thickness, and e is electron charge.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Hall-effect sensors for isolated, bidirectional current measurement with fast response and low power loss.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power dissipation in current sensors?
faqs.answer: Power dissipation in Allegro current sensors comes from the conductor resistance: P = I² × R_conductor. Allegro sensors have very low conductor resistance (0.3-1.5mΩ typical), minimizing power loss. Example calculations: ACS712 (1.2mΩ): at 10A, P = 100 × 0.0012 = 0.12W; at 30A, P = 900 × 0.0012 = 1.08W. ACS37610 (0.1mΩ): at 100A, P = 10000 × 0.0001 = 1W; at 200A, P = 40000 × 0.0001 = 4W. Thermal considerations: Junction temperature Tj = Ta + (P × θja), where Ta is ambient temperature and θja is thermal resistance (typically 40-60°C/W for SOIC packages). Ensure Tj stays below maximum (usually 150°C for automotive grade). For high-current applications, provide adequate copper area on PCB for heat spreading. Some high-current sensors include thermal shutdown protection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate P = I² × R; ensure Tj < 150°C with proper thermal design; use low-R sensors for high-current applications.

faqs.question: What bandwidth do I need for my current sensing application?
faqs.answer: Current sensor bandwidth selection depends on application requirements: DC applications (battery monitoring, power supplies): 0-10kHz bandwidth sufficient; AC line monitoring (50/60Hz): 1-10kHz bandwidth; Motor control (PWM switching): 50-100kHz to capture PWM ripple; Inverter output monitoring: 10-50kHz for fundamental frequency; Fast fault detection: > 100kHz for overcurrent protection; High-frequency power conversion: 500kHz-1MHz for GaN/SiC converters. Allegro offers sensors from DC to 1MHz bandwidth. Higher bandwidth provides faster response but may introduce more noise. For most motor control applications, 50-100kHz is adequate. For fast protection circuits, choose > 100kHz. The ACS37010 offers 1MHz bandwidth for high-frequency applications. Always verify that sensor bandwidth exceeds your signal bandwidth requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 50-100kHz for motor control; > 100kHz for fast protection; 1MHz for high-frequency power conversion.

faqs.question: How do I interface Allegro current sensors with microcontrollers?
faqs.answer: Allegro current sensors offer multiple output options for microcontroller interfacing: Analog voltage output (ACS712, ACS720): Output is proportional voltage (e.g., 185mV/A for ACS712-05B), connect directly to ADC input; use voltage divider if ADC range is lower than sensor output. PWM output (ACS37800): Duty cycle proportional to current, measure with timer/capture input. Digital I2C/SPI (ACS37800, ACS37010): Direct digital interface, read current value via serial bus. Key considerations: Reference voltage - sensors may output ratiometric to Vcc or fixed reference; ADC resolution - calculate LSB size to ensure adequate current resolution; Sampling rate - sample at > 2× bandwidth for accurate capture; Filtering - add RC filter at sensor output to reduce noise (typically 1-10kHz cutoff). For high-noise environments, use differential ADC inputs or add external instrumentation amplifier.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use analog output for simple applications; digital I2C/SPI for noisy environments; PWM for isolated digital interface.

faqs.question: What accuracy can I expect from Allegro current sensors?
faqs.answer: Allegro current sensor accuracy varies by product and conditions: Total error at 25°C: ±1% to ±5% depending on device; Total error over temperature (-40°C to 150°C): ±2% to ±8%; Offset error: ±5mV to ±30mV depending on device; Sensitivity error: ±1% to ±3%; Nonlinearity: ±0.5% to ±2%. Factors affecting accuracy: Temperature - all sensors have temperature drift, use devices with internal compensation; External magnetic fields - shield or orient to minimize interference; Power supply variation - use stable Vcc for best accuracy; PCB layout - follow guidelines for optimal performance. For highest accuracy applications (energy metering), use ACS720 with ±1% total error. For general monitoring, ACS712 with ±2-3% is sufficient. Always check datasheet for specific accuracy specifications at your operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ±1% sensors for metering; ±2-3% for monitoring; consider temperature drift for automotive applications.

products.name: ACS712 Fully Integrated Hall-Effect Current Sensor
products.shortDescription: Economical Hall-effect current sensor with 2.1kV isolation, 5A/20A/30A ranges, and 80kHz bandwidth for AC/DC current sensing.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACS712 is a fully integrated Hall-effect current sensor that provides economical and precise AC or DC current sensing. The device consists of a precise, low-offset linear Hall circuit with a copper conduction path located near the surface of the die.

When current flows through the copper conduction path, the resulting magnetic field is sensed by the integrated Hall IC and converted to a proportional voltage. The device is packaged in a SOIC-8 package with internal isolation providing 2.1kV RMS dielectric strength.

The ACS712 is available in three current ranges: ±5A (185mV/A), ±20A (100mV/A), and ±30A (66mV/A). The output is ratiometric to the supply voltage and has a zero-current output of Vcc/2, allowing bidirectional current measurement.

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Current Sensing
faeReview.content: The ACS712 is my go-to recommendation for cost-sensitive current sensing applications. At around $1-2 in volume, it offers excellent value for basic current monitoring. The 2.1kV isolation is adequate for most low-to-medium voltage applications up to 400V working voltage. I've used it successfully in motor drives, power supplies, and battery management systems. The ratiometric output simplifies interface to 5V ADCs. Key limitations: 80kHz bandwidth limits it to lower-frequency applications - don't use for high-frequency inverter monitoring. The ±1.5% accuracy is good for monitoring but not precise enough for energy metering. Temperature drift is noticeable - expect ±3-4% over the full automotive range. For higher accuracy, step up to the ACS720. Overall, an excellent entry-level current sensor for non-critical applications.
faeReview.highlight: Excellent value at $1-2; 2.1kV isolation adequate for most apps; ratiometric output simplifies ADC interface

alternativeParts.reason: Higher accuracy and isolation for demanding applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Upgrade path for designs requiring better accuracy or higher isolation

alternativeParts.reason: Digital interface with integrated diagnostics for advanced applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Applications requiring digital interface and advanced fault detection

companionParts.description: Full-bridge motor driver for current-controlled motors
LED driver for load monitoring applications
Linear Hall sensor for position feedback

faqs.question: What is the maximum working voltage for ACS712?
faqs.answer: The ACS712 provides 2.1kV RMS isolation voltage between the current path and sensor circuitry. The maximum working voltage (continuous) is determined by safety agency standards and is typically 400-600V depending on pollution degree and overvoltage category. The basic insulation rating is 2.1kV RMS for 60 seconds. For reinforced insulation (touch-safe applications), derate to 600V working voltage. The isolation barrier is tested to 4.2kV peak for 60 seconds during production. For higher working voltages, consider the ACS720 with 4.8kV isolation. Always check the specific safety certifications (UL, CSA, IEC) for your application requirements and ensure compliance with local electrical safety regulations.
faqs.decisionGuide: 2.1kV isolation supports 400-600V working voltage; use ACS720 for higher voltages; check safety certifications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the output voltage for a given current?
faqs.answer: The ACS712 output voltage is calculated as: Vout = Vcc/2 + (I × Sensitivity). For bidirectional sensing, Vcc/2 is the zero-current output (2.5V with 5V supply). For example, ACS712-05B (±5A, 185mV/A): At +5A: Vout = 2.5V + (5 × 0.185) = 3.425V; At -5A: Vout = 2.5V + (-5 × 0.185) = 1.575V; At 0A: Vout = 2.5V. The sensitivity is ratiometric to Vcc, so at 3.3V supply, sensitivity scales to 122mV/A (185 × 3.3/5). For ADC interface: Current = (Vadc - Vcc/2) / Sensitivity. Include offset calibration for best accuracy by measuring output at 0A and subtracting from readings.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Vout = Vcc/2 + (I × Sensitivity); sensitivity is ratiometric to Vcc; calibrate offset at 0A.

faqs.question: What is the noise level of ACS712 and how do I filter it?
faqs.answer: The ACS712 has output noise of approximately 7mV RMS (21mV peak-to-peak) with 80kHz bandwidth. This corresponds to current noise of: ACS712-05B: 7mV / 185mV/A = 38mA RMS; ACS712-30A: 7mV / 66mV/A = 106mA RMS. To reduce noise, add an RC low-pass filter at the output: Cutoff frequency fc = 1 / (2πRC). For 1kHz cutoff with 1kΩ resistor: C = 1 / (2π × 1000 × 1000) = 159nF, use 150nF standard value. Trade-off: lower bandwidth means slower response. For DC measurements, use 10-100Hz cutoff. For AC line monitoring, use > 100Hz cutoff. The internal bandwidth can also be limited by adding capacitor between FILTER pin and ground (if available on variant). For lowest noise, average multiple ADC samples in software.
faqs.decisionGuide: 7mV RMS noise; add RC filter for noise reduction; trade-off between noise and bandwidth.

faqs.question: Can ACS712 measure AC current?
faqs.answer: Yes, the ACS712 can measure AC current with its 80kHz bandwidth. The output is a sine wave centered at Vcc/2 (2.5V for 5V supply). For AC measurements: Peak current = (Vpeak - 2.5V) / Sensitivity; RMS current = Peak / √2. Example: ACS712-05B measuring 3A RMS: Peak = 3 × √2 = 4.24A; Vpeak = 2.5V + (4.24 × 0.185) = 3.28V; Vmin = 2.5V - (4.24 × 0.185) = 1.72V. The output swings between 1.72V and 3.28V at the AC frequency. For microcontroller interface: Sample at > 2× AC frequency (e.g., > 100Hz for 50Hz line); Calculate RMS by averaging squared samples; Or use peak detection circuit. The 80kHz bandwidth supports AC measurements up to several kHz, making it suitable for inverter output monitoring and motor current sensing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Yes, 80kHz bandwidth supports AC; output centered at Vcc/2; sample at > 2× frequency for RMS calculation.

faqs.question: What is the temperature drift of ACS712?
faqs.answer: The ACS712 has temperature drift specifications: Sensitivity drift: ±0.5% to ±1.5% over -40°C to 150°C depending on grade; Offset drift: ±5mV to ±15mV over temperature; Total error: ±2% to ±4% over temperature range. The ECO grade (E suffix, -40°C to +85°C) has better drift characteristics than the automotive grade (K suffix, -40°C to +150°C). For highest accuracy over temperature: Use ECO grade for industrial applications; Implement temperature compensation in software using external temp sensor; Perform calibration at multiple temperatures; Use ACS720 for better temperature performance (±1% over temp). Example impact: At 100°C with 2% drift, a 10A measurement could read 9.8-10.2A. For applications requiring < 2% accuracy over temperature, consider the ACS720 or implement calibration.
faqs.decisionGuide: ±2-4% drift over temperature; use ECO grade for better specs; implement calibration for highest accuracy.

products.name: ACS37610 High-Current Busbar Current Sensor
products.shortDescription: High-precision current sensor for busbar mounting with ±200A range, 1MHz bandwidth, and 4.8kV isolation for EV applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACS37610 is a high-precision, high-bandwidth current sensor designed for busbar mounting in high-current applications such as electric vehicle battery management and high-power motor drives. The sensor features Allegro's proprietary Hall-effect technology with advanced signal processing.

With a sensing range up to ±200A and 1MHz bandwidth, the ACS37610 can accurately measure both DC and high-frequency AC currents. The device provides 4.8kV isolation voltage, making it suitable for high-voltage EV battery packs up to 800V.

The sensor offers ±1% accuracy at 25°C with integrated temperature compensation maintaining accuracy across the automotive temperature range. The differential Hall sensing architecture rejects external magnetic fields, providing reliable measurements in noisy electromagnetic environments.
features: Differential Hall sensing for noise immunity
Busbar mounting for low thermal resistance

faeReview.author: David Park
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Automotive Systems
faeReview.content: The ACS37610 is my top recommendation for high-current EV applications. The 1MHz bandwidth is exceptional - I've used it to monitor SiC inverter switching with excellent results. The busbar mounting is a game-changer for thermal management; with proper copper area, we've measured < 50°C temperature rise at 200A continuous. The differential sensing really works - we saw < 1% error even with 1000A/cm external fields nearby. The 4.8kV isolation gives plenty of margin for 800V EV systems. One consideration is the custom footprint - you'll need to design your busbar specifically for this sensor. Also, the differential output requires a differential ADC or instrumentation amplifier, adding some complexity. But for high-current, high-voltage applications, the performance is unmatched. The AEC-Q100 Grade 0 qualification is essential for automotive. Overall, the best high-current sensor I've worked with.
faeReview.highlight: 1MHz bandwidth excellent for SiC inverters; busbar mounting provides superior thermal performance; differential sensing rejects external fields

alternativeParts.reason: Lower current range with standard SOIC package for easier PCB mounting
alternativeParts.useCase: Applications up to 100A requiring standard PCB mounting

alternativeParts.reason: Lower cost and easier mounting for moderate current applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Cost-sensitive applications up to 40A not requiring high bandwidth

companionParts.description: Automotive BLDC motor driver for EV pumps
Matrix LED driver for EV lighting
Angle sensor for motor position feedback

faqs.question: How do I mount the ACS37610 on a busbar?
faqs.answer: The ACS37610 requires custom mechanical mounting on a busbar: 1) Design busbar with flat area for sensor mounting; 2) Ensure clean, flat surface for good thermal contact; 3) Use thermal interface material (TIM) between sensor and busbar; 4) Apply uniform mounting pressure using screws or clips; 5) Torque screws to specification (typically 0.5-1.0 Nm); 6) Ensure no air gaps for optimal heat transfer. Thermal design: The sensor dissipates P = I² × R_conductor (typically < 0.1mΩ). At 200A, P = 4W. With thermal resistance of 5°C/W (typical with good mounting), temperature rise is 20°C. Maintain ambient + rise < 150°C. PCB connections: Route sense and power connections away from high-current busbar. Use twisted pairs for differential output. Keep high-current paths short and wide. Allegro provides mechanical drawings and thermal simulation guidelines.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use TIM for thermal contact; apply uniform pressure; ensure good thermal design for high-current operation.

faqs.question: What is the differential output of ACS37610?
faqs.answer: The ACS37610 provides differential analog output for noise immunity: Vout_diff = Vout+ - Vout- = Sensitivity × I. The outputs are centered around common-mode voltage (typically Vcc/2). For ±200A range with 10mV/A sensitivity: At +200A: Vout+ = 2.5V + 1.0V = 3.5V, Vout- = 2.5V - 1.0V = 1.5V, Vdiff = 2.0V; At 0A: Vout+ = Vout- = 2.5V, Vdiff = 0V; At -200A: Vout+ = 1.5V, Vout- = 3.5V, Vdiff = -2.0V. Interface options: Differential ADC - connect Vout+ and Vout- directly to differential inputs; Single-ended ADC - use instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620) to convert differential to single-ended; Or sample both outputs separately and subtract in software. Differential signaling provides: Rejection of common-mode noise; Better EMI immunity; Longer cable runs possible. For best performance, route differential traces as twisted pair or closely coupled differential traces.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use differential ADC or instrumentation amp; route as twisted pair; provides excellent noise immunity.

faqs.question: What is the overcurrent detection feature?
faqs.answer: The ACS37610 includes a fast overcurrent detection (OCD) output: Function - digital output goes low when current exceeds programmed threshold; Response time - < 1μs from overcurrent to OCD output; Threshold - programmable via external resistor or digital interface; Hysteresis - prevents chatter near threshold; Use cases - fast fault protection for IGBTs/MOSFETs, short-circuit detection, current limiting. Configuration: Set threshold resistor Rth = (Vcc × Rint) / (Vth - Voffset), where Rint is internal reference, Vth is desired threshold voltage. Or program digitally via I2C/SPI if available. Connection: Connect OCD output to microcontroller interrupt or directly to gate driver shutdown. The OCD operates independently of the analog output, providing redundant protection. Example: For 150A overcurrent threshold with 10mV/A sensitivity: Vth = 150A × 10mV/A = 1.5V. Configure accordingly.
faqs.decisionGuide: Program threshold based on protection requirements; < 1μs response for fast protection; use for gate driver shutdown.

faqs.question: Can ACS37610 measure high-frequency ripple current?
faqs.answer: Yes, the ACS37610's 1MHz bandwidth enables high-frequency ripple current measurement: Capability - accurately measure ripple up to ~500kHz (Nyquist criterion); Applications - SiC/GaN inverter output monitoring, switching power supply ripple, motor drive current ripple; Measurement - the sensor captures both DC component and AC ripple; Output - analog output follows ripple waveform in real-time; For digital systems, sample at > 2× ripple frequency. Example: SiC inverter switching at 100kHz: The sensor captures the 100kHz switching ripple superimposed on DC current; With 1MHz bandwidth, amplitude error is minimal (< 3dB). For analysis: Use oscilloscope to view ripple waveform; Calculate ripple amplitude and RMS; Verify inverter operation and filter performance. The high bandwidth is a key advantage over lower-bandwidth sensors (80-120kHz) that would attenuate high-frequency components.
faqs.decisionGuide: 1MHz bandwidth captures ripple to 500kHz; ideal for SiC/GaN inverter monitoring; sample at > 2× ripple frequency.

faqs.question: What external magnetic field rejection does ACS37610 provide?
faqs.answer: The ACS37610 uses differential Hall sensing for excellent external field rejection: Architecture - two Hall elements sense magnetic field from opposite sides of conductor; Common-mode rejection - external fields affect both sensors equally and cancel out; Rejection ratio - typically > 40dB (100:1) for uniform external fields; Performance - < 1% error with 1000A/cm external fields. Test conditions: Place sensor near high-current conductor (e.g., adjacent phase in 3-phase system); External field from adjacent conductor can be 100-500 Gauss; Differential sensing reduces error to < 1% of full scale. Limitations: Non-uniform fields (e.g., nearby magnet) may not cancel completely; Best practice - maintain distance from strong magnetic sources; Use magnetic shielding if necessary for extreme environments. The differential architecture is a significant advantage over single-element sensors for applications in high-field environments like motor drives and power converters.
faqs.decisionGuide: Differential sensing provides > 40dB external field rejection; < 1% error in high-field environments; ideal for motor drives.
